query: cases of foreign bodies migrating passively to the heart

retrieved_docs.title: Gastrointestinal Foreign Body -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The foreign body, when ingested, can lodge in the GI tract resulting in complete or partial obstruction. Furthermore, it can erode the GI mucosal wall and lead to migration and perforation. This is more likely to be seen in patients with prior GI diseases such as eosinophilic esophagitis, gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), congenital GI anomalies, and neuromuscular disorders. The most common site for obstruction is at the thoracic inlet (at the level of clavicles and cricopharyngeus muscle). 10% to 15 % lodge at the level of carina and aortic arch and rest at the esophagogastric junction. The foreign bodies which pass through the stomach do not cause any problem. However, some may get trapped at the ileocecal valve, causing problems. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Airway Foreign Bodies(Archived) -- Consultations
retrieved_docs.content: Interventional pulmonology, otolaryngology, cardiothoracic surgery

retrieved_docs.title: Body Fluid Exposures -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: On physical exam, patients need to be examined at the site of the percutaneous exposure or the site of exposure. The area needs to be examined for foreign bodies, bleeding, or signs of localized infection. If splashed into an eye, the eye should be examined for any obvious foreign bodies, debris, or signs of trauma. An exam of the heart, lungs, abdomen and lymph nodes should be done as a baseline for possible infection later on. Most exposures will not exhibit clinical signs until later. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Cardiac Trauma -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Foreign bodies visible externally on patient arrival and suspected to be related to a penetrating cardiac injury should be left in place temporarily to allow for set-up for operative evaluation and care. Otherwise, retained foreign bodies should be removed operatively if contamination or electrical abnormalities exist. [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Airway Foreign Bodies(Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: If the patient is stable and cooperative, evaluation can begin with chest X-rays, PA, and lateral inspiratory-expiratory films. Unilateral expansion with diaphragm flattening of the affected side with mediastinal pushing away will indicate obstruction on that side. Air trapping can also be demonstrated on inspiratory-expiratory films indicative of a ball-valve effect due to a foreign body obstruction. If the foreign body is radiopaque, these can also identify and localize the object. Obtain IV access with bloodwork including complete blood count and electrolytes in preparation for a possible general anesthetic. A routine venous blood gas is of little use and should be avoided. If the patient is stable and cooperative, a head and neck exam with or without flexible laryngoscopy can elucidate other mimicking conditions such as tonsillitis or peritonsillar abscess, which can then be addressed. Auscultation in the primary lung fields can demonstrate wheezing or focal consolidations when paired with percussion and tactile fremitus evaluation. Computed tomography should be reserved for only the most stable and comfortable patients in whom foreign body is very low on the differential diagnosis. An airway emergency in a radiology suite carries a high mortality. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Foreign Body Ingestion -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Esophageal obstruction is the most frequently encountered complication in pediatric foreign body ingestion. [34] In rare cases, certain foreign bodies may perforate the alimentary tract or migrate into surrounding structures, resulting in life-threatening outcomes, including the following: Esophageal, gastric, or bowel wall perforation Pneumothorax Mediastinitis Mucosal erosion Gastrointestinal bleeding Aortoenteric fistula Esophageal or bowel wall pressure necrosis Failure to thrive and dehydration secondary to decreased oral intake Prompt identification and intervention remain critical in preventing serious complications. Although severe outcomes are rare, delayed recognition may lead to devastating consequences.

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Foreign Body Ingestion -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis depends on the severity of resulting complications. Children who pass objects spontaneously tend to recover fully without lasting effects. In contrast, cases involving severe outcomes such as mediastinitis from esophageal perforation, aortoenteric fistula, or bowel perforation with peritonitis require a cautious outlook and close follow-up.

retrieved_docs.title: Percutaneous Central Catheter(Archived) -- Complications -- Catheter Malposition/Migration
retrieved_docs.content: Migration is a change in the length of the catheter extruding from the insertion site. When the catheter tip migrates spontaneously after initial placement in a proper location, it is referred to as secondary malposition . This can occur with high-frequency ventilation, extreme physical activity, and rapid infusion/forceful flushing of fluid. Migration interferes with the ability to withdraw blood. However, it can also cause cardiac tamponade via erosion of the catheter through the RA or ventricle. This is a medical emergency and must be addressed immediately.

retrieved_docs.title: Foreign Body Airway Obstruction -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: According to the National Safety Council’s statistics, FBAO is the 4th leading cause of unintentional death, with 5051 documented fatalities in 2015. FBAO is a leading cause of accidental deaths in children younger than 16. [2] All persons, including those outside of the health field, should have a basic understanding of how to care for a choking victim due to the prevalence and rapidity of unconsciousness and death from this condition. Simple maneuvers taught to laypeople, such as the Heimlich maneuver, have been proven to save lives. [3] Complete FBAO is immediately life-threatening, while partial FBAO can hinder gas exchange and result in dyspnea, pneumonia, and abscess formation. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Foreign Body -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: A disc battery impacted in the esophagus is a true emergency and needs immediate removal. The greatest concern is the potentially fatal complication of an aortoesophageal fistula with the highest risk in children less than five years old, battery size 20 mm or greater, impaction at the aortic arch level, prolonged impact, and any degree of hematemesis. In these specific cases, an interprofessional approach may be indicated, potentially including pediatric gastroenterology, pediatric surgery, cardiothoracic surgery, anesthesia, and radiology with management in the operating room or cardiac catheterization lab. Asymptomatic children with a coin impacted in the esophagus can be managed urgently with the observation of up to 24 hours without risk of further complications. Coin location is important, with 10% of proximal esophageal coins, 26% of middle esophagus coins, and 43% of distal esophagus coins passing spontaneously within 16 hours of ingestion.

retrieved_docs.title: Gastrointestinal Foreign Body -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Parents should be educated about the dangers of leaving magnets and batteries easily accessible to small children around the house. They should be made aware of the necessity to be seen promptly if there is a suspicion of ingestion of one of these objects. If a child is asymptomatic following the ingestion of a coin or small object, parents can be educated that monitoring at home and inspecting the stool for 10 to 14 days is an acceptable response. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Gastrointestinal Foreign Body -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: It is important to remember that if patients are presenting with symptoms, such as chest pain, abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting, a more extensive workup, including labs, should be initiated to rule out alternative diagnoses. If patients present with suspected acute esophageal obstruction, imaging is not necessary to localize and should not delay urgent endoscopy.

retrieved_docs.title: Human Trafficking -- History and Physical -- Head-to-Toe Assessment
retrieved_docs.content: Signs of chest trauma, murmurs; cigarette burns; tattoos that imply ownership; bruising in various stages of healing; signs of stress-related cardiovascular issues such as arrhythmias or high blood pressure.

retrieved_docs.title: Foreign Body Airway Obstruction -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis of FBAO depends on the degree of obstruction and duration of hypoxia. Patients with partial FBAO who can clear the airway often experience few to no complications and may be treated based on their risk factors for future aspiration events. Meanwhile, loss of consciousness typically occurs within seconds or minutes in complete FBAO. Outcomes are bleak in patients requiring CPR, as mortality reaches 90% for out-of-hospital cardiac arrests. Mortality reaches 60% to 70% for individuals who survive hospital admission. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Foreign Body Aspiration(Archived) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: According to one study, when a combination of three or more highly suggestive clinical and radiological diagnostic data were noted, the risk of identifying a foreign body in the airway was 91%. Diagnostic clinical criteria significantly correlated with the presence of a foreign body included: a history of sudden choking, cyanosis, apnea, and decreased breath sounds. Diagnostic radiological criteria included: atelectasis, mediastinal shift, or signs of air trapping.  The absence of the aforementioned criteria was very predictive of a negative flexible bronchoscopy. Thus, it was deemed safe to refer these patients for outpatient follow-up as opposed to initial bronchoscopy. [6] [11] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Fascioliasis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Fasciola’s migration out of the intestine and up into the liver is not always perfect; sometimes they get lost. Ectopic fascioliasis is the term given to the infection occurring outside the hepatobiliary system. They may end up almost anywhere in the body. Their presence results in localized mononuclear, eosinophilic infiltration that causes secondary tissue damage from the host’s immune response. Frequently, when they get lost, they end up in the subcutaneous tissue of the abdominal wall, but they may also end up in the peritoneum, intestinal wall, lungs, heart, brain, muscles, or eyes. Traveling flatworms under the skin or fat tissue leave behind migrating, pruritic, erythematous, painful nodules 1 cm to 6 cm in diameter and may be seen or palpated. Occasionally nodules can become infected and turn into a local abscess. [1] [12] [13] [14] [15] Another rare form of ectopic fascioliasis is Halzoun syndrome or pharyngeal fascioliasis. It is sometimes seen in the Middle East in areas where people eat raw liver. In this case, the metacercariae or young adult flukes living in the ingested liver attach themselves to the mucosa of the upper respiratory or digestive tract causing pharyngitis or esophagitis. Rarely edema and swelling of the upper airway can be so severe that patients have asphyxiated. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 1 -- Community 177
retrieved_docs.content: The community maps a detailed cardiovascular taxonomy that links heart anatomy, pathology, systemic risk factors, and a broad spectrum of diagnostic, monitoring, and therapeutic interventions. Cardiac pacing, demand pacing and temporary cardioassist with an artificial heart are classified as cardiovascular procedures; the latter also belongs to HEART: GENERAL AND MISCELLANEOUS OPERATIVE PROCEDURES, a group that includes open‑heart surgery and diagnostic procedures on the heart (NOS). Cardioversion by intracardiac catheter, elective external electrical cardioversion, whole‑body plethysmography and cardiopulmonary resuscitation (إنعاش قلبي رئوي) are child interventions whose parent procedure is heart biopsy (خزارة القلب). retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 143
retrieved_docs.content: The community organizes cardiac interventions within a hierarchical framework that begins with the broad category of Cardiovascular and Respiratory System Procedures, under which Cardiovascular procedures are listed as a sub‑section of Operations and procedures. Cardiac pacing is defined as a specific cardiovascular procedure and is grouped with other therapeutic and diagnostic actions such as Cardiovascular examination and evaluation, Cardioversion by intracardiac catheter, elective electrical cardioversion of arrhythmia (external), Demand pacing, Temporary cardioassist with artificial heart, whole‑body plethysmography, and open‑heart massage. The node HEART: GENERAL AND MISCELLANEOUS OPERATIVE PROCEDURES, a child of other or heart procedures, serves as a parent for Temporary cardioassist with artificial heart, Operation on heart and pericardium, open‑heart surgery (جراحة القلب المفتوح), and Diagnostic procedure on heart, NOS; the latter is a specific type of Procedure on heart.

retrieved_docs.content: Cardiovascular procedures constitute a broad hierarchical group of heart‑related interventions that are children of the general “Operations and procedures” category and are linked to the “heart‑biopsy” (خزبة القلب) domain. This group includes therapeutic techniques such as cardiac pacing, demand pacing, cardioversion (both intracardiac catheter‑based and elective external electrical), temporary cardioassist with artificial heart, whole‑body plethysmography, and open‑heart massage, as well as diagnostic actions like heart‑specific diagnostic procedures and plethysmography. Within the sub‑category “HEART: GENERAL AND MISCELLANEOUS OPERATIVE PROCEDURES,” temporary cardioassist with artificial heart, operation on heart and pericardium, open‑heart surgery (جراحة القلب المفتوح), and “Diagnostic procedure on heart, NOS” are organized, the latter being an inverse ISA of “Procedure on heart” to emphasize its diagnostic nature. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 230
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiovascular procedures form a central hub within the broader Operations and Procedures hierarchy and are linked to a parent grouping of heart‑related procedures (خزعة القلب). This category encompasses therapeutic and diagnostic techniques such as cardiac pacing, demand pacing, intracardiac‑catheter and elective external cardioversion, temporary cardioassist with an artificial heart, whole‑body plethysmography, open‑heart massage, and cardiovascular examination and evaluation. Cardiac pacing is classified as a “procedure by method,” emphasizing technique over anatomical target. The subcategory Cardiovascular and Respiratory System Procedures is a child of Cardiovascular procedures, integrating respiratory‑related interventions. Within the HEART: GENERAL AND MISCELLANEOUS OPERATIVE PROCEDURES branch—itself a child of the “other or heart procedures” classification—temporary cardioassist with an artificial heart reappears, highlighting its cross‑taxonomic relevance. Diagnostic cardiac procedures comprise diagnostic cardiac catheterization, coronary arteriography, and a broad set of “Other diagnostic procedures on heart and pericardium.” Heart biopsy (خزعة القلب) is the parent procedure for open left‑ventricular biopsy, open right‑ventricular biopsy and transluminal left‑ventricular biopsy, and it anchors the category HEART: EXCISIONS, which also lists excision of a heart valve. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 773
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac pacing is classified as a cardiovascular procedure that is defined by its method of delivery, indicating that it belongs to the broader group of cardiovascular procedures. Cardiovascular procedures themselves are a subcategory of the larger “Cardiovascular and Respiratory System Procedures” hierarchy, serving as a parent node for a diverse set of specific interventions. These child procedures include cardiovascular examination and evaluation, cardioversion performed with an intracardiac catheter, elective external electrical cardioversion for arrhythmia conversion, demand pacing, temporary cardioassist with an artificial heart, whole‑body plethysmography (pletyzmografie celotělová), and open‑heart massage (تدليك القلب المفتوح). In addition, the “HEART: GENERAL AND MISCELLANEOUS OPERATIVE PROCEDURES” category also lists temporary cardioassist with an artificial heart as one of its child procedures and encompasses operative interventions on the heart and pericardium. Together, these relationships illustrate a structured taxonomy in which general cardiovascular procedural categories branch into method‑specific techniques and specialized therapeutic or diagnostic actions, facilitating organized clinical documentation, education, and patient‑care planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 1 -- Community 151
retrieved_docs.content: The community defines a hierarchical taxonomy of cardiac and cardiovascular interventions. At the highest level, Cardiovascular and Respiratory System Procedures encompass Cardiovascular procedures, which include diagnostic and therapeutic actions such as cardiovascular examination and evaluation, elective external cardioversion for arrhythmia conversion, demand pacing, cardiac pacing, temporary cardioassist with an artificial heart, full‑body plethysmography (pletyzmografie celotělová), and cardiopulmonary resuscitation (إنعاش قلبي رئوي) in both mechanical and non‑mechanical forms. Cardiovascular procedures are linked to cardiac biopsy (خزبة القلب) as a foundational parent, positioning biopsy techniques as a basis for many interventions. Cardiac valvotomy is associated with valve excision (استئصال تأموري), indicating that valvotomy may involve removal of valvular tissue. The operative branch “HEART: GENERAL AND MISCELLANEOUS OPERATIVE PROCEDURES” is a child of “Operation on heart and pericardium” and includes temporary cardioassist with an artificial heart and diagnostic procedure on heart, NOS, which is a type of Procedure on heart. Open‑heart surgery (جراحة القلب المفتوح) resides within the same operative group. HEART: EXCISIONS is a subcategory of heart biopsy (خزبة القلب) and comprises excisional procedures such as excision of heart valve and specific ventricular biopsies. Open left ventricular biopsy and open right ventricular biopsy are instances of heart biopsy and are also classified as forms of open‑heart surgery. Together, these relationships illustrate a multi‑layered framework that connects diagnostic, evaluative, pacing, resuscitation, valvular, and excisional techniques to their broader procedural categories, providing a comprehensive structure for clinical understanding and patient care.
